speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Felicia Carmelly, maiden name, Steigman. Tape five.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was caught in the fire. I managed to roll in the grass outside. And I didn't have the-- I wasn't burned too badly. The woman, whose name I forgot, wasn't there. She was at the hospital with her mother. But she was punished. And I don't remember in what way. And I was sent, as a punishment, to the dump outside of the city of Bucharest, where they were bringing in trucks of garbage and unloading the garbage. That meant working in a toxic environment. I wore very high rubber boots. And I was in the truck shoveling down the-- the-- the garbage. Most of the people who worked there were gypsies, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] illiterate-- really a totally different society that-- that I was used to. But, again, I organized literacy classes for them and stuff. And when I came home from there, because I only worked seven hours a day instead of eight, because it was a toxic environment, I would soak in the bathtub for an hour. By the way, that was not easy, because this bathtub was shared with a large number of people on that floor. And then I would dress in the best clothes I had, and go out to opera or to theater, which was on a very high standard. In Romania, it was state supported then. It was-- I think that's what kept me alive. Until we went to Israel, and then we went to Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, back to Montreal. I arrived in Montreal with my husband in 1963. I immediately started to learn English. There were no English as a Second Language classes there. It was sink or swim. You do it on your own. And I learned English pretty quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to university. I went to McGill. And they recognized some of my papers from Israel and some of my papers from Romania. So I only had to do two years for a master's degree in social work, which to me, at that time, seemed the closest thing to medicine, which I wanted to do when I finished high school. And medical school was not accessible to me when I came to Montreal, because of the length of time and because of the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came, like I said, with my husband, whose name was Martin Carmelly. I'd known him. We were friends in Romania. But we weren't really dating. We were just friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we married in Israel. And 1964, my daughter was born, and her name is Ramona Joy Carmelly. And Carmelly is my name. I chose the name in Israel, and it comes from Mount Carmel. And my husband took my name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after I finished McGill, I took a job at Jewish Family and Child Services in Montreal, and I organized some very interesting programs. Because I was such a good student and I was so active, I was sent by the community to study at the School of Jewish Communal Service in Los Angeles. I was there two summers. And that's the same school which also trains rabbis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then I came to-- I came to Montreal-- to Toronto in 1972. I was brought here by Jewish Family and Child Services to organize a similar program that I've organized in Montreal, which was a family life education program in schools for students, parents, and teachers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then I worked at-- I was program director at Holy Blossom Temple and organized the project, which was a pilot project for all reform synagogues in North America. And I was very successful. Then I worked for 12 years for the federal government as a counselor. During that time, in 1985, I took my PhD by correspondence from California. And I, basically during all this time, put in-- when my daughter was 2 and 1/2 years old, I divorced. And so I lived as a single mother. And I put all my energy into raising my daughter and developing my career. And I never talked about the camps. And it was a chapter of my life which I had put aside, and-- and it seemed to me that I'll never get back to it. I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: You put it aside, but did it affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course it affected my life. I continued to have nightmares. And I continued to think about it. But I didn't talk about it to other people, except to my daughter and to my husband. And my father died in Israel. And then my mother came to Toronto. And she did not want to talk about it. So-- and outside-- in the outside world, I didn't want to talk about it, because, at the beginning, coming from the camps was a negative stigma to the population here. And that was one reason why I didn't talk about it. And the second was because I felt that nobody would understand anyway. And I didn't want to waste energy on that, because I needed all the energy I had available to raise my daughter and to build my house and to build my career and to make it in this world. However, in 1994, I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] found a book that my present husband, whose name is Bill, bought and hid from me, because it was a Holocaust book. And that book opened up this whole chapter for me. And I started talking about Transnistria. And realized that nobody here knew, or has ever heard of, Transnistria, which made me very angry. Because there were half a million people who died there, between the local Ukrainians and the Romanians. And I was very angry that nobody knew about it. And when I started to do some research, I realized that there was absolutely nothing in the English language about it, and very little in other languages, too. So I decided to form an association, which was called the Transnistria Survivors Association in Toronto. I was also helping people organize similar groups in Montreal and in other cities. And eventually, we decided that it's important to write a book about this. And since I was one of the younger ones, and I had a better command of the English language, and I was prepared to put my life on hold for this book, I was the one who wrote the book, which was published recently. And it is called, The book has a lot of documentation. It has about 200 pages of history, and about 300 pages of personal testimonies from survivors of Transnistria, who presently live in Russia, Romania, Israel, the United States, Canada, and Venezuela. The book has terrific feedback. And now I have to start promoting it, and marketing it, and selling it, because the purpose of writing the book is to let the story be known.

speaker: interviewer
text: By writing this book, did it help you deal with the issues of your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it, first of all, it helped me deal with-- it helped me understand the whole picture of what Transnistria was all about. Because my perspective of it was that of a 10-year-old kid, or 12-year-old, or 13 years old, which was-- I was aware of what's happening in the immediate vicinity of my hut or in the hut. But I really didn't have a complete historic picture of what this-- this terrible thing was all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by doing research for the book, I understood what it was and where my place in it was. And it was cathartic in many ways, because I sort of got it out of my system, which also made it very difficult to write this book. And I had to write in record time, because people were pressuring me to get the book, and because I got so involved with the book that I felt that either I finish the book or the book finishes me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband was also starting to get a little anxious, because he said, Transnistria didn't kill you then, but it's killing you now. So, do it. Finish it. And I finished it. And I'm very proud of that. And sometimes I think that maybe that's why I survived, because somebody had to write the story of Transnistria, and it happened to be me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to leave the next generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, that prejudice is a terrible poison. And that it has to be avoided. Kids have to be educated to know what hatred to other people-- racial or religious, what it-- what-- what a terrible-- what terrible acts it can lead to. Another message is that even though there were many more collaborators with the Nazis in all the European countries, including Transnistria, than people who helped, another important message, for me, is to have the courage to stand up for what they believe, even if it-- even if it involves some risk. Because a lot of people were saved as a result of people having the courage to help and to hide people who were less fortunate, and to bring food, or to give them false papers, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Felicia, why did you want to share your story with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, because the most-- the two of the most challenging projects in my life were being an interviewer for the Shoah Foundation myself. And I felt that I should do it, too. Also because I want Transnistria to be known through this tape, too. And also because, if and when I'll have grandchildren, I want them to know what their background is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to share with us today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad that I did the interview. And it was a lot less difficult for me to do it than I thought it would be. And I think that this is primarily because I had written the book, and I got a lot out of my system through the book. And I'm glad it's over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a picture of my grandmother, Rebecca Siegler, or Rivka, her Hebrew name, and her three daughters. She is at the extreme right. Next to her in the back is my mother, Lipzia. Siegler, her maiden name and Steigman, her married name. Next to her is her middle sister, Sidi, who today lives in Israel. And her name is Sidi Rauchweger. And then the front is Mila Siegler, whose married name was Treiser. And she died in Montreal in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken, I think, in the mid '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dornei where we lived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my parents, Laura Siegler and Isaac Steigman. It is a picture of their engagement taken in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vatra-Dornei. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandmother, Rebecca Siegler. The picture was taken in 1932, and she's holding me in her arms. And I'm a few months old. This is a picture taken in Vatra-Dornei. And I look like I'm about four or five years old, so it must be in the mid '30s. To the left is my mother, Laura Steigman. And then it's me. And on the other side of me is a cousin of my mother's whose name is [? Yesel ?] [? Mueller, ?] who died during the war in Russia. And his son and his family have [? emigrated ?] a few years ago from Russia, and they now live in Israel. In the back of the picture is one of the bridges across the river, Dorna, and there is a sign up there which reads-- [SPEAKING ROMANIAN], which in Romanian-- which in English means the road of life. This is a picture of my mother, Laura Steigman, and myself. I am about four years old, I think. And the location of this picture was taken in Belz when we visited my aunt Sidi, who married somebody who lived there. This is a picture of my uncle Armin. His family name is Treiser. In front of him is sitting his wife, my aunt, Mila Treiser. Next to her are Murray Treiser and Diana Treiser. And in the back of Diana Treiser is my Aunt Sidi Rauchweger, who now lives in Israel. The picture was taken in Montreal in the late '50s soon after they [? emigrated ?] from Belgium to Canada. And my aunt Sidi just came for a visit. This is a picture of my parents, Isaac and Laura Steigman. The picture was taken in Montreal on the occasion of my cousin, Murray Treiser's, bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the '70s. This is a picture-- this picture was taken in 1994 in Toronto. The lady on the left is my mother, Laura Steigman. And hanging on to her is Diana Cogan, who lives in Washington. And next to her is her brother, Murray Treiser, who lives in New Jersey. And they came to visit my mother. This was one year before my mother died. This is a picture of my cousin, Diana Cogan's two daughters. To the right is Jessica Cogan. And to the left is Mia Cogan. And they live in New York. This is an old picture of my cousin, Murray Treiser's two sons. To the right is Adam Treiser. And to the left is Matthew Treiser. Today Adam is 14, and Matthew is 17. This picture was taken in Toronto, 1995. That's myself and my husband, Bill Cox. This is a picture of my only daughter, Ramona Joy Carmelly. She lives in Toronto, and she's an opera singer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken 1993 or '94. This is my book, which was recently published, called This book represents to me a memorial to the innocent victims which died in Romania [? and ?] the [? programs, ?] which I didn't mention, and in Transnistria. And the flame symbolizes-- a flame, as well, and it's made up of the letters which represent the word, Yizkor, which is the prayer for the dead.
